assertion_results.text: Output identifies the $17,000 surrender charge as a major red flag and calculates whether the new product's benefits would overcome that cost over a reasonable time horizon
assertion_results.evidence: Red Flag #3 (lines 105-121) identifies '$17,000 surrender charge loss' as a major red flag, calculates it as '~7.9% of account value', and states: 'For a replacement to be in her best interest, the new product must provide $17,000+ in measurable, documented benefit over what her current product provides. That is an extremely high bar.' Asks: 'Can you quantify, in guaranteed values (not hypothetical), how the new product produces $17,000+ more benefit?'

assertion_results.text: Output explicitly warns about the replacement regulations that apply (state replacement forms, notification to existing carrier, comparison documentation) rather than just processing the sale
assertion_results.evidence: Section 6 (lines 345-373) provides a detailed replacement compliance checklist including: 'State replacement form — completed and signed', 'Side-by-side comparison of existing FIA vs. proposed product' with six sub-items, 'Notice to existing carrier — most states require you to notify the existing insurance company of the replacement', 'Written justification explaining why the replacement is in Margaret's best interest despite the $17,000 loss', and 'Compensation disclosure'.

assertion_results.text: Output questions whether a 71-year-old should be entering a new 7-10 year surrender period and discusses age-appropriate surrender period considerations
assertion_results.evidence: Red Flag #5 (lines 146-156) states: 'At 71, a new 10-year surrender period means Margaret cannot access her money without penalty until age 81. Even a 7-year surrender period extends to age 78.' Lists state senior protections that may 'Prohibit surrender periods beyond a certain length for clients over 65 or 70'. Red Flag #4 adds: 'She's 71 years old. A new 10-year surrender period annuity would lock her money up until age 81... At her age, liquidity and access are critical.'

assertion_results.text: Output performs or recommends a suitability analysis comparing her income ($2,100 SS + RMD income) against expenses ($4,200) to determine if she actually needs a different product or if the current one is adequate
assertion_results.evidence: Section 2 (lines 30-48) performs an income gap analysis: 'Monthly essential expenses: $4,200', 'Guaranteed income (Social Security): $2,100/month', 'Monthly income gap: $2,100/month = $25,200/year'. Then asks: 'Does Margaret actually have an income problem, or does she have an allocation/education problem? Her existing FIA may already have an income rider that could fill this gap.' Section 4 (lines 172-191) provides a full suitability table with annual income '$25,200 SS + RMD income' and the $2,100/mo gap.

assertion_results.text: Output addresses the 'neighbor recommended it' element as a red flag — the recommendation should come from professional analysis, not informal advice, and the agent must independently determine suitability
assertion_results.evidence: Red Flag #2 (lines 86-103) states: '"My neighbor told me about a product with a higher cap rate" is a classic trigger for unsuitable replacements. Neighbors are not licensed financial professionals. A higher cap rate on a new product does NOT mean better performance.' Lists six specific ways a higher cap rate can be misleading. Action: 'Do NOT chase cap rates.' Conversation framework (lines 430-435) models how to redirect: 'I appreciate that your neighbor was trying to help. But comparing annuity products is complex.'

assertion_results.text: Output discusses the alternative of waiting until the surrender period expires on the existing annuity (6 more years) rather than taking the $17,000 loss now
assertion_results.evidence: Step 5 (lines 286-288) explicitly recommends: 'Do NOT replace the FIA. Let it ride for the remaining 6 years of the surrender period. At that point, it can be 1035 exchanged with no penalty.' Proposes addressing the income gap with the IRA instead.

assertion_results.text: Output mentions that 'isn't performing well' for an FIA may be a misunderstanding — the 0% floor means no loss years, and the client may not understand how index crediting works, requiring education rather than replacement
assertion_results.evidence: Red Flag #1 (lines 57-82) extensively covers this: 'Margaret says her FIA "isn't performing well." This is the single most common misunderstanding with Fixed Indexed Annuities.' Explains cap rates, participation rates, and the 0% floor: 'she lost nothing while the S&P 500 may have dropped 15-25%.' FIAs 'capture a portion of upside in exchange for eliminating downside risk.' Action: 'Educate before recommending any change.' Step 3 (lines 260-261) provides client-facing language: 'Your annuity earned 0% in down years — that means you didn't lose money. That IS the product working correctly.'

assertion_results.text: Output warns against moving 100% of assets into a new annuity and recommends maintaining her $50,000 savings as liquid emergency funds
assertion_results.evidence: Red Flag #4 (lines 124-143) calls consolidating all assets 'a massive suitability failure'. States: 'Never put more than 50-60% of a client's liquid assets into annuities.' Specifies: 'Her $50,000 savings account is her emergency fund and should NOT go into an annuity under any circumstances.' Summary table (line 458): 'Touch the $50K savings — NO — This is her emergency fund; must remain liquid.' Step 5 (line 292): 'Protect the savings account. The $50,000 stays liquid — period.'
